subject: Biology
unit: Unit Five: The human body systems

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.subsection: 5.1.3. The human axial and appendicular skeletons
questions.question: Which of the following is a component of the axial skeleton?
questions.hint: Think of the central axis of the body, not the limbs.
questions.explanation: The axial skeleton includes the skull, vertebral column, and rib cage (thorax). Options A, C, and D are part of the appendicular skeleton (limbs and girdles).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 5.1.3. The human axial and appendicular skeletons
source_trace.content_anchor: consists of the skull, trunk, and pelvis bones of the medial core of the body

explanations_tiered.definition: The axial skeleton forms the central axis of the body, including the skull, vertebral column, and rib cage.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: It protects the brain, spinal cord, and thoracic organs, and provides attachment for the appendicular skeleton.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The axial skeleton is the 'core' of your body — your skull, backbone (spine), and ribs.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The axial skeleton comprises 80 bones: 22 skull bones, 26 vertebrae, 24 ribs, and the sternum.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The axial skeleton develops from the paraxial mesoderm (somites), whereas the appendicular skeleton derives from lateral plate mesoderm.
reasoning_steps: Recall that axial skeleton = head and trunk.
Identify rib cage as part of the trunk.
Eliminate limb bones (femur, humerus) and pelvis (appendicular).
misconceptions: Some think the pelvis is axial; it is actually part of the appendicular skeleton (pelvic girdle).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Axial skeleton: skull, spine, ribs, sternum (80 bones).

glossary_entry.definition: Central skeleton including skull, vertebral column, and rib cage.

flashcard.front: What bones make up the axial skeleton?
flashcard.back: Skull, vertebral column (spine), and rib cage (sternum + ribs).

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The rib cage is part of the axial skeleton; femur, humerus, and pelvis are appendicular.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: Which sequence correctly represents the order of the four phases of the menstrual cycle?
questions.hint: The cycle begins with bleeding, then builds up, releases an egg, and then prepares for pregnancy.
questions.explanation: Menstruation (days 1-4) is followed by the follicular phase (days 5-13), then ovulation (day 14), and finally the luteal phase (days 15-28). Option A is incorrect because menstruation occurs before the follicular phase.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: Menstrual Phase 1-4, Follicular Phase 5-13, Ovulation phase 14, Luteal Phase 15-28

explanations_tiered.definition: The menstrual cycle has four phases in order: menstruation, follicular, ovulation, and luteal.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The cycle begins with endometrial shedding, then follicle growth, egg release, and finally corpus luteum formation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The cycle starts with your period (menstruation), then the body builds up the egg (follicular), releases it (ovulation), and finally waits (luteal).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Day 1-4: menstruation (shedding). Days 5-13: follicular (FSH rise, estrogen increases). Day 14: ovulation (LH surge). Days 15-28: luteal (progesterone dominates).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The luteal phase is relatively fixed at 14 days; variation in cycle length is due to the follicular phase. The luteal phase ends with corpus luteum regression.
reasoning_steps: Recall that menstruation occurs first.
Ovulation occurs mid-cycle.
Luteal phase follows ovulation, before the next menstruation.
misconceptions: Some think ovulation starts the cycle; actually menstruation is day 1, ovulation is around day 14.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cycle order: Menstruation → Follicular → Ovulation → Luteal.

glossary_entry.definition: Monthly reproductive cycle: menstruation, follicular, ovulation, luteal phases (average 28 days).

flashcard.front: What is the order of the four phases of the menstrual cycle?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Menstruation is the start of the cycle (day 1), then follicular, ovulation (mid-cycle), then luteal.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: The developing human is called a fetus after which week of gestation?
questions.hint: Recall the embryonic period ends at 8 weeks.
questions.explanation: After the eighth week of gestation, the developing human is called a fetus. Before 8 weeks, it is an embryo. Option B (12 weeks) is after the fetal period has begun.

calculation_steps: Standard embryology: embryonic period = first 8 weeks; fetal period = week 9 to birth.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems

explanations_tiered.definition: The fetal period begins at 8 weeks of gestation, after the embryonic period ends.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The embryo becomes a fetus when most major organ systems have formed; further development involves growth and maturation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: At 8 weeks of pregnancy, the baby is called a fetus, meaning it has all major body parts formed.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The embryonic period (weeks 1-8) involves organogenesis; after week 8, the organism is a fetus and continues growing until birth.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The transition from embryo to fetus occurs at approximately 56 days post-fertilization, corresponding to 70 days gestational age (LMP).
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall that the embryonic period = first 8 weeks.
Step 2: Understand that after 8 weeks, the term changes to fetus.
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of embryo vs fetus.
Embryonic period lasts 8 weeks.
At 8 weeks, it becomes a fetus.
misconceptions: Some think the term 'fetus' starts at 12 weeks; the correct transition is at 8 weeks.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Fetus: from 8 weeks gestation (after embryonic period).

glossary_entry.definition: Developing human from the end of the 8th week of gestation until birth.

flashcard.front: At what gestational week does the embryo become a fetus?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The embryonic period is weeks 1-8; from week 9 onward it is a fetus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.subsection: 5.4.1 Risks related to the lack of family planning
questions.structural_anchor: 5.4.1 Risks related to the lack of family planning
questions.question: Short birth intervals (less than 18 months between pregnancies) increase the risk of adverse outcomes for both mother and child. Which of the following is a direct consequence for the newborn?

options.B: Increased risk of preterm birth and low birth weight

questions.hint: Think about the mother's nutritional and physiological recovery time.
questions.explanation: Short interpregnancy intervals deplete maternal nutritional stores and are associated with higher rates of preterm birth and low birth weight. Option A is incorrect; short intervals are linked to lower birth weight, not higher.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 5.4.1 Risks related to the lack of family planning
source_trace.structural_element: 5.4.1 Risks related to the lack of family planning
source_trace.content_anchor: Both early and late motherhood have increased risk that teenagers could face a higher risk of life complications and deaths

explanations_tiered.definition: Short interpregnancy intervals (under 18 months) increase risks of preterm birth and low birth weight.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Maternal nutritional depletion (folate, iron) and incomplete uterine involution are contributing factors.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a mother has babies too close together, her body doesn't have time to recover, so the next baby may be born too early or too small.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Short interpregnancy intervals are associated with a 40-60% increased risk of preterm birth and a 30-50% increased risk of low birth weight.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mechanisms include folate deficiency (neural tube defects), depletion of maternal fat stores, and chronic inflammation from short recovery time.
reasoning_steps: Recall that short interpregnancy intervals cause maternal depletion.
Identify that this leads to preterm birth and low birth weight.
Eliminate opposite outcomes (higher birth weight).
misconceptions: Some think having babies close together is easier; actually it increases risks for both mother and child.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Short birth interval (<18 months) → preterm birth + low birth weight.

glossary_entry.definition: Time between a live birth and the next conception; short intervals (<18 months) increase risks.

flashcard.front: What are two newborn risks of short birth intervals (<18 months)?
flashcard.back: Preterm birth and low birth weight.

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.4.1 Risks related to the lack of family planning
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Short intervals increase preterm and low birth weight risk; they do not increase birth weight.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.subsection: 5.2.6 The major sexually transmitted infections (STIs) in Ethiopia
questions.question: In Ethiopia, the highest reported rates of STIs are found among which age group?
questions.hint: Think about the most sexually active age range and risk behaviors.
questions.explanation: In Ethiopia, the highest incidence of STIs occurs among 15-24 year olds due to factors such as multiple partners, inconsistent condom use, and lack of access to services. Option A is incorrect as STIs are rare in young children except for vertical transmission.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 5.2.6 The major sexually transmitted infections (STIs) in Ethiopia
source_trace.content_anchor: In Ethiopia, the highest reported rates of STIs are found among 15-24 year olds

explanations_tiered.definition: Epidemiology of STIs in Ethiopia shows the highest burden in adolescents and young adults (15-24 years).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This age group accounts for a disproportionate share of new infections due to behavioral, biological, and access-related factors.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In Ethiopia, young people aged 15-24 get STIs more often than any other age group.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Young adults aged 15-24 represent 50% of new STI cases in Ethiopia, driven by early sexual debut, multiple partners, and low condom use.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Young women have higher biological susceptibility due to cervical ectopy; structural barriers include lack of youth-friendly health services and stigma.
reasoning_steps: Recall the age group with highest STI incidence in Ethiopia.
Identify 15-24 year olds from the PDF.
Eliminate other age ranges.
misconceptions: Some think STIs are most common in older adults; globally and in Ethiopia, young people have the highest rates.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: STIs in Ethiopia: highest in 15-24 year olds.

glossary_entry.definition: Study of disease patterns, causes, and distribution in populations.

flashcard.front: Which age group in Ethiopia has the highest rate of STIs?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The PDF explicitly states the highest rates are in 15-24 year olds.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.section: 5.5 Effects of alcohol use, chewing Khat, cannabis and other drug uses
questions.question: A person with chronic alcohol use disorder develops cardiomyopathy (heart muscle disease). Some might attribute this only to poor nutrition. What is the direct toxic effect of alcohol on the heart muscle?

options.B: Alcohol and its metabolite acetaldehyde directly damage cardiac myocytes
options.C: Alcohol only affects the liver, not the heart

questions.hint: Think about alcohol's direct cellular toxicity.
questions.explanation: Chronic alcohol exposure directly damages heart muscle cells via oxidative stress and acetaldehyde toxicity, leading to dilated cardiomyopathy. Option A is incorrect; while moderate alcohol may raise HDL, heavy use does not benefit the heart.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.section_anchor: 5.5 Effects of alcohol use, chewing Khat, cannabis and other drug uses
source_trace.content_anchor: Drinking over a long time damages the heart and causes cardiomyopathy

explanations_tiered.definition: Alcoholic cardiomyopathy is caused by direct toxicity of ethanol and its metabolite acetaldehyde on cardiac myocytes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Chronic exposure leads to myocyte apoptosis, mitochondrial dysfunction, and reduced contractility, often presenting as heart failure.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Alcohol doesn't just harm the liver — it also directly poisons heart muscle cells, making the heart weak and floppy.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Acetaldehyde impairs calcium handling and myofilament sensitivity; oxidative stress induces cardiac myocyte apoptosis and fibrosis.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Alcoholic cardiomyopathy is dose-dependent; even after cessation, recovery may be incomplete, and it is a leading cause of non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy.
reasoning_steps: Recall that alcohol is metabolized to acetaldehyde, a toxic compound.
Identify that acetaldehyde directly damages cardiac muscle cells.
Understand that this leads to cardiomyopathy, not just nutritional deficiency.
misconceptions: A common misconception is that only nutritional deficiencies (e.g., thiamine) cause alcoholic heart disease; alcohol has direct toxic effects independent of nutrition.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Alcohol → acetaldehyde → direct cardiac myocyte damage → cardiomyopathy.

glossary_entry.definition: Heart muscle disease caused by direct toxic effects of alcohol and acetaldehyde, leading to heart failure.

flashcard.front: How does alcohol directly damage the heart muscle?
flashcard.back: Ethanol and its metabolite acetaldehyde are directly toxic to cardiac myocytes.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Acetaldehyde and oxidative stress directly damage myocytes; the effect is not only from malnutrition.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.section: 5.5 Effects of alcohol use, chewing Khat, cannabis and other drug uses
questions.question: Cocaine use increases the risk of stroke through which two main mechanisms?
questions.hint: Think about cocaine's effects on blood pressure and brain blood vessels.
questions.explanation: Cocaine causes a surge in blood pressure (hypertension) and induces cerebral vasospasm (constriction of brain arteries), both of which can lead to ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke. Option A is opposite; cocaine causes tachycardia and hypertension, not hypotension.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.section_anchor: 5.5 Effects of alcohol use, chewing Khat, cannabis and other drug uses
source_trace.content_anchor: Cocaine harms the cardiovascular system, breathing system, gastrointestinal system, and nervous system

explanations_tiered.definition: Cocaine-induced stroke results from sudden severe hypertension and cerebral vasospasm caused by sympathetic overactivity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Intense vasoconstriction reduces cerebral blood flow, while hypertension can rupture weakened vessels.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Cocaine makes blood pressure spike dangerously high and squeezes the brain's arteries shut, which can cause a stroke.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Cocaine blocks norepinephrine reuptake, causing unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation; this leads to hypertensive surges and reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hemorrhagic stroke risk is increased by cocaine-induced aneurysmal rupture; ischemic stroke results from vasospasm and increased platelet aggregation.
reasoning_steps: Recall that cocaine increases sympathetic activity.
Identify that this causes hypertension and vasospasm.
Both mechanisms increase stroke risk.
misconceptions: Some think cocaine only affects the heart; it also causes cerebral vasospasm and hemorrhagic stroke.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cocaine → hypertension + cerebral vasospasm → stroke risk ↑.

glossary_entry.definition: Constriction of brain arteries, often caused by cocaine, reducing blood flow and causing stroke.

flashcard.front: How does cocaine increase the risk of stroke?
flashcard.back: It causes severe hypertension and cerebral vasospasm (artery constriction).

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Hypertension can rupture vessels (hemorrhagic stroke), and vasospasm reduces blood flow (ischemic stroke).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.section: 5.5 Effects of alcohol use, chewing Khat, cannabis and other drug uses
questions.question: In addition to respiratory depression, long-term heroin use is associated with which chronic medical condition due to infectious complications from needle sharing?
questions.hint: Think of blood-borne infections from sharing needles.
questions.explanation: Intravenous heroin users who share needles are at high risk for infective endocarditis (heart valve infection) and hepatitis C (liver infection). Option A is not directly caused by needle sharing; option C is autoimmune.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.section_anchor: 5.5 Effects of alcohol use, chewing Khat, cannabis and other drug uses
source_trace.content_anchor: People suffering from drug and alcohol addiction have a higher risk of unintentional injuries, accidents, and domestic violence incidents

explanations_tiered.definition: Injection drug use, particularly heroin, leads to blood-borne infections including hepatitis C and HIV, as well as infective endocarditis.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Contaminated needles introduce bacteria and viruses directly into the bloodstream, causing systemic infections.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Sharing needles to inject heroin can spread serious infections like hepatitis C (liver disease) and endocarditis (heart valve infection).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Hepatitis C affects 50-80% of long-term injection drug users; endocarditis is usually caused by Staphylococcus aureus from skin contamination.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Needle exchange programs reduce hepatitis C and HIV incidence; infective endocarditis in IV drug users often affects the tricuspid valve.
reasoning_steps: Identify that needle sharing transmits pathogens.
Recall common blood-borne infections: hepatitis C, HIV, and bacterial endocarditis.
Select the option containing endocarditis and hepatitis C.
misconceptions: Some think heroin's dangers are only from overdose and addiction; infectious complications from needle sharing are major causes of morbidity and mortality.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: IV heroin (needle sharing) → endocarditis + hepatitis C.

glossary_entry.definition: Infection of the heart valves or endocardium, often caused by bacteria from contaminated needles.

flashcard.front: What infections are common in IV heroin users who share needles?
flashcard.back: Infective endocarditis (heart valves) and hepatitis C (liver).

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Infective endocarditis and hepatitis C are classic needle-sharing complications; diabetes and arthritis are not directly caused.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.subsection: 5.2.6 The major sexually transmitted infections (STIs) in Ethiopia
questions.sub_subsection: 5.2.7.1 Types of sexually transmitted infections and preventions
questions.question: Hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection can be prevented by a vaccine that provides immunity against the viral surface antigen (HBsAg). Which of the following body fluids is the primary vehicle for sexual transmission of HBV?
questions.hint: Think about which fluids are exchanged during sexual contact.
questions.explanation: HBV is present in high concentrations in blood, semen, and vaginal secretions, making sexual transmission efficient. Option A (saliva) may contain the virus but is not the primary sexual transmission vehicle.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 5.2.6 The major sexually transmitted infections (STIs) in Ethiopia
source_trace.content_anchor: Hepatitis B ... once a person is infected, the virus remains in the semen, blood and other bodily fluids

explanations_tiered.definition: Hepatitis B virus is transmitted sexually through semen, vaginal fluids, and blood, with high infectivity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: HBV is 50-100 times more infectious than HIV; vaccination provides >95% protection.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Hepatitis B spreads through sexual contact because the virus lives in semen and vaginal fluids, not just blood.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: HBV is present in semen at concentrations up to 10⁸ copies/mL; vaginal secretions also contain infectious virus.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The HBV vaccine contains recombinant HBsAg; post-exposure prophylaxis includes vaccine and hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG).
reasoning_steps: Recall that HBV is sexually transmitted.
Identify the fluids involved in sexual transmission: semen and vaginal secretions.
Eliminate other body fluids not primarily associated with sexual transmission.
misconceptions: Some think HBV is only transmitted through blood (like HIV); it is actually more efficiently transmitted sexually than HIV.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: HBV sexual transmission: semen, vaginal secretions.

glossary_entry.definition: Viral hepatitis transmitted sexually and parenterally; preventable by vaccine.

flashcard.front: What body fluids transmit hepatitis B sexually?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Semen and vaginal secretions are the primary fluids for sexual transmission; saliva and sweat are not major routes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.section: 5.5 Effects of alcohol use, chewing Khat, cannabis and other drug uses
questions.question: A teenager is found unconscious with a can of aerosol spray next to them. Paramedics note that the heart rhythm is ventricular fibrillation. Which mechanism explains this fatal arrhythmia from inhalant abuse?
questions.hint: Think about how inhalants make the heart hyper-reactive to stress hormones.
questions.explanation: Volatile solvents sensitize the myocardium to catecholamines (adrenaline), lowering the threshold for ventricular fibrillation, especially during exercise or startle. Option A (liver toxicity) is a chronic effect, not immediate cause of fatal arrhythmia.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.section_anchor: 5.5 Effects of alcohol use, chewing Khat, cannabis and other drug uses
source_trace.content_anchor: Inhalants are chemicals that can be found in ordinary household or workplace products

explanations_tiered.definition: Cardiac sensitization is the increased susceptibility of the heart to catecholamine-induced arrhythmias, caused by volatile inhalants.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This mechanism is responsible for 'sudden sniffing death', where a startle or exercise triggers ventricular fibrillation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Inhalants make the heart dangerously sensitive to adrenaline; even a sudden loud noise can cause the heart to stop beating properly.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Solvents block potassium channels (hERG) and increase sodium channel activity, prolonging QT interval and facilitating re-entry arrhythmias.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The sensitization threshold can be as low as 0.1% atmospheric concentration; physical exertion or emotional stress precipitates fatal arrhythmias.
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify that the teen inhaled a volatile solvent.
Step 2: Recognize ventricular fibrillation as the fatal arrhythmia.
Step 3: Recall that inhalants cause cardiac sensitization to catecholamines.
reasoning_steps: Recall that inhalants cause 'sudden sniffing death'.
Understand that this is due to cardiac sensitization.
Recognize that sensitization leads to ventricular fibrillation with catecholamine surges.
misconceptions: Many think inhalant deaths are from suffocation; while that can occur, sudden sniffing death from arrhythmia is a distinct mechanism and can happen with first use.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Inhalants → cardiac sensitization → VF → sudden death.

glossary_entry.definition: Increased susceptibility to catecholamine-induced arrhythmias caused by volatile solvents, leading to sudden death.

flashcard.front: What is the mechanism of 'sudden sniffing death' from inhalants?
flashcard.back: Cardiac sensitization to catecholamines, causing ventricular fibrillation.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Cardiac sensitization (increased sensitivity to adrenaline) triggers ventricular fibrillation; liver toxicity is chronic.
